classification: no longer considered safe as a food additive
verbatim_quote: It was banned as a food additive by the European Union in 2022, after the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) raised concerns about its safety.
jurisdiction: Permitted in Malaysia; banned as a food additive in the EU since 2022.

classification: flour treatment agent the FDA urges bakers not to use
verbatim_quote: Since 1991, the FDA has urged bakers not to use it, but has not mandated a ban.
jurisdiction: Banned in the EU, UK and Canada; discouraged in the US; still used in some Malaysian bakery flour.

classification: emulsifier banned from food and drink in several countries
verbatim_quote: Canada has banned the use of brominated vegetable oil since August 30, 2024.
jurisdiction: Banned in Canada (2024), the US (2024) and the EU; may still appear in some citrus soft drinks.

classification: flour/dough conditioner banned as a food additive in some regions
verbatim_quote: In jurisdictions such as Australia and the European Union , azodicarbonamide as a food additive is banned.
jurisdiction: Banned as a food additive in the EU and Australia; permitted (limited) in the US and Malaysia.

classification: reasonably anticipated to be a human carcinogen
verbatim_quote: National Institutes of Health report that BHA is reasonably anticipated to be a human carcinogen based on evidence of carcinogenicity in experimental animals.
jurisdiction: Permitted as an antioxidant in Malaysia and the US; flagged as a suspected carcinogen and restricted elsewhere.

classification: synthetic antioxidant preservative
verbatim_quote: It is permitted in the European Union under E321
jurisdiction: Permitted in the EU, US and Malaysia; restricted or avoided by some manufacturers over safety concerns.

classification: curing agent linked to carcinogenic nitrosamines
verbatim_quote: has been classified as "probably carcinogenic to humans" by International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC).
jurisdiction: Permitted as a curing preservative in Malaysia, the EU and US; IARC links nitrite/processed meat to cancer.

classification: curing preservative similar to sodium nitrite
verbatim_quote: potassium nitrite is used as a preservative similar to sodium nitrite
jurisdiction: Permitted as a curing agent in Malaysia and the EU; shares the nitrosamine concern of sodium nitrite.

classification: azo dye associated with hyperactivity and intolerance
verbatim_quote: Tartrazine is listed as a permitted food coloring in Canada
jurisdiction: Permitted in Malaysia, EU and US, but the EU requires a hyperactivity warning label the US does not.

classification: azo dye subject to restriction and warning labelling
verbatim_quote: Sunset yellow FCF was not approved in Norway before 2001.
jurisdiction: Permitted in Malaysia, EU and US, but the EU requires a hyperactivity warning label the US does not.

classification: azo dye that must carry a warning label in the EU
verbatim_quote: In the European Union, it is approved, but it must carry a warning label.
jurisdiction: Permitted in Malaysia and the US; approved in the EU only with a mandatory hyperactivity warning label.

classification: azo dye not approved for food use in the US
verbatim_quote: used in Europe, Asia, and Australia, but has not been approved for human consumption by the United States
jurisdiction: Permitted in Malaysia and the EU (with warning label); not approved for food in the US.

classification: colour not permitted as a food additive in the US or Canada
verbatim_quote: Quinoline Yellow is not listed as a permitted food additive in Canada or the US
jurisdiction: Permitted in Malaysia and the EU (with warning label); not a permitted food additive in the US or Canada.

classification: azo dye delisted for food use in the US
verbatim_quote: It was delisted in 1963 because no party was interested in supporting the studies needed to establish safety.
jurisdiction: Permitted in Malaysia and the EU (with warning label); not approved for food in the US.

classification: preservative that can form benzene with ascorbic acid
verbatim_quote: In 2006, the Food and Drug Administration tested 100 beverages available in the United States that contained both ascorbic acid and benzoate.
jurisdiction: Permitted in Malaysia, the EU and US; can form benzene in acidic drinks containing vitamin C.

classification: sweetener IARC classifies as possibly carcinogenic
verbatim_quote: The FDA disagrees with IARC's conclusion that these studies support classifying aspartame as a possible carcinogen to humans.
jurisdiction: Permitted in Malaysia, the EU and US; IARC classified it as possibly carcinogenic (Group 2B) in 2023.

classification: artificial sweetener under ongoing safety review
verbatim_quote: In the European Union, it is known under the E number (additive code) E950
jurisdiction: Permitted in Malaysia, the EU and US; EFSA has been asked to re-evaluate its safety.

classification: flavour enhancer permitted subject to quantitative limits
verbatim_quote: The European Union classifies it as a food additive permitted in certain foods and subject to quantitative limits.
jurisdiction: Permitted in Malaysia and the US; the EU sets a group ADI and quantitative limits on its use.

classification: added-sugar sweetener restricted by EU production quota
verbatim_quote: In the European Union (EU), HFCS is known as isoglucose or glucose–fructose syrup ( GFS )
jurisdiction: Widely used in the US; production in the EU was capped by isoglucose quotas until 2017.

classification: thickener restricted in infant formula in the EU
verbatim_quote: The use of carrageenan in infant formula is not prohibited in the EU for precautionary reasons, but is permitted in other food items
jurisdiction: Permitted in Malaysia, the EU and US; the EU applies precautionary limits for infant formula.

classification: paraben preservative removed from EU food additives
verbatim_quote: As of May 2023, New York was considering banning the use of propylparaben
jurisdiction: No longer permitted as a food additive in the EU; still used in some products in the US and elsewhere.

classification: preservative capped at differing limits by jurisdiction
verbatim_quote: The upper limit of total SO 2 allowed in wine in the US is 350 ppm; in the EU it is 160 ppm for red wines and 210 ppm for white and rosé wines.
jurisdiction: Permitted as a preservative in Malaysia, the EU and US, but the EU sets much lower limits than the US.

classification: sweetener banned in the US but permitted in the EU
verbatim_quote: Safety concerns led to it being banned in a few countries, though the European Union considers it safe.
jurisdiction: Permitted in Malaysia and the EU; banned as a food additive in the US since 1969.

classification: sweetener formerly listed as a suspected carcinogen
verbatim_quote: countries such as Canada have lifted their previous ban of it as a food additive
jurisdiction: Permitted in Malaysia, the EU and US; was banned in Canada and carried a US warning label for decades.

classification: colour banned from many US uses over carcinogenicity
verbatim_quote: in all uses as its lake variant have been banned by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) since 1990
jurisdiction: Permitted in Malaysia and the EU; the US banned the lake form in 1990 and revoked remaining food uses in 2025.

classification: antioxidant restricted or banned in some countries
verbatim_quote: The FDA recognizes propyl gallate as being generally safe, but in other countries, it's either been used on a very limited basis or completely banned.
jurisdiction: Generally recognised as safe in the US; restricted or banned as a food additive in some other countries.

classification: widely permitted food preservative
verbatim_quote: used as a food preservative ( E number 202).
jurisdiction: Permitted as a preservative in Malaysia, the EU and US; among the more benign synthetic preservatives.

classification: industrial trans fat banned from the US food supply
verbatim_quote: First, in 2013 the FDA announced it planned to completely ban artificial trans fat in the form of partially hydrogenated oil.
jurisdiction: Industrial trans fat (partially hydrogenated oil) banned in the US and restricted by the WHO; still found in some Malaysian products.

classification: synthetic blue colour approved in the US and EU
verbatim_quote: It is approved for human consumption in the United States and European Union.
jurisdiction: Permitted in Malaysia, the EU and US, but the EU requires a hyperactivity warning label the US does not.

classification: synthetic green colour not permitted for food in the EU
verbatim_quote: In the United States, Fast Green FCF is the least used of the seven main FDA approved dyes.
jurisdiction: Approved for food in the US; not permitted as a food colour in the EU.

classification: synthetic blue colour with cross-country label differences
verbatim_quote: It is denoted by E number E133 and has a color index of 42090.
jurisdiction: Permitted in Malaysia, the EU and US; the EU requires a hyperactivity warning label the US does not.

classification: azo dye banned as a food colour in the US
verbatim_quote: amaranth dye has been banned in the United States by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA)
jurisdiction: Permitted in Malaysia and the EU (with warning label); banned as a food colour in the US since 1976.

classification: azo dye suspended as a food colour in the EU
verbatim_quote: In the European Union, Red 2G was used as a food dye ( E number E128).
jurisdiction: Suspended as a food colour in the EU since 2007 over safety concerns; never approved in the US.

classification: fat substitute approved in the US but not the EU
verbatim_quote: approved olestra for use in the US as a replacement for fats and oils in prepackaged
jurisdiction: Approved as a food additive in the US; not authorised in the EU, UK or Canada.
